summary: Joel Spolsky writes a tribute to Adam Bosworth, praising him as one of the most influential software designers in the industry. He traces Bosworth's career from Microsoft Access through ODBC, Internet Explorer's DOM, SOAP, and XML. Joel uses Bosworth's example to illustrate the kind of talent Fog Creek Software aspires to attract, and urges readers to carefully study Bosworth's recent writing on n-tier architectures and the real-world limitations of SOAP. The post also touches on how seemingly minor UX problems—like the inability to show progress indicators in web browsers—can have outsized business consequences.
key_points: Adam Bosworth exemplifies the ideal program manager: fast-thinking, consistently correct, but also willing to listen and change his mind without ego
Bosworth's track record of shipping world-shaping software (Access, ODBC, IE's DOM, SOAP, XML) is unmatched
The typical SOAP demos showing stock quote services are unrealistically simplistic and don't reflect real-world scaling challenges
Small UX failures like missing browser progress indicators during long server tasks can threaten entire industries
Fog Creek Software's hiring ambition is explicitly benchmarked against attracting people of Bosworth's caliber
money_quotes: He is an incredibly fast thinker and incessantly right.
Unlike most fast-thinkers who are always right, he also knows how to listen, and if he discovers he was wrong, he'll change his mind without emotional attachment to his old ideas.
Adam has designed and shipped more world-shaping software than anyone else I can think of.
One of the goals of my own company, Fog Creek Software, is to create a place where we stood a snowflake's chance in hell of getting superstars like Adam Bosworth to work.
Understand why all the six hundred demos you've seen of a SOAP service that returns stock quotes are unrealistically simplistic and don't scale up to the real world.

Think about how the fact that it's almost impossible to display a progress indicator in a web browser while the server does some 2 minute task came as close to killing the entire online travel-agency industry as Nikita Khrushchev came to destroying the world by putting missiles in Cuba.
key_insight: The best software leaders combine relentless intellectual speed with genuine openness to being wrong, and even tiny UX shortcomings in platforms can have industry-threatening consequences.
